coredata.dc:title: Dual-signal model array sensor based on GQDs/AuNPs system for sensitive protein discrimination
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xin
Hai, Xin
Wang, Ning
Chen, Xu-Wei
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: The accurate differentiation and identification of proteins play a vital role in many areas. Herein a novel array sensor is developed for sensitive discrimination of proteins, based on the various optical responses of GQDs/AuNPs system towards different protein species. The simultaneously generated distinct variations of fluorescence and absorbance of GQDs/AuNPs system resulted from the interactions between protein species and sensing units contribute to a dual-signal strategy for protein discrimination. The protein concentration for complete discrimination is low down to 50 nM, and accurate discriminations of protein mixture of different concentrations/molar ratio are achieved. The complementary fluorescence and absorbance response makes this dual-signal model array sensor practicable to sample of complicated matrices, demonstrated by the accurate discrimination of protein species in human urine. Moreover, six strains of microorganisms originated from three different species are also successfully discriminated with 100% accuracy (OD600 = 1.0).
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
AuNPs
Dual-signal model
Array sensor
Protein discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Differentiation and identification of protein species
Differentiation and identification of different proteins
Differentiation and identification of unknown protein samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of AuNPs (A) and GQDs (B). The top and bottom insets display the HRTEM image and size distribution, respectively.
Mechanism for protein discrimination by GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor.

(A) LDA plot for the discrimination of six proteins (500 nM) by the GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor; (B) Fingerprints of six selected proteins (500 nM) based on the patterns of the corresponding values of (I−I0)/I0 obtained from the fluorescence and absorbance; (C, D) LDA plot for the discrimination of six proteins (C: 100 nM; D: 50 nM) by the GQDs/AuNPs-based array sensor.
LDA plot for the discrimination of (A) Hb and BSA at various concentrations; (B) protein mixtures (BSA and Hb, total concentration of 500 nM) by the GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor.
LDA plot for the discrimination of proteins (500 nM) in healthy human urine by the GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor.
LDA plot for the discrimination of 6 strains of microorganisms (OD600 = 1.0) by the GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor.
Basic properties of the tested protein species.

head.ce:title: Dual-signal model array sensor based on GQDs/AuNPs system for sensitive protein discrimination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate differentiation and identification of proteins play a vital role in many areas. Herein a novel array sensor is developed for sensitive discrimination of proteins, based on the various optical responses of GQDs/AuNPs system towards different protein species. The simultaneously generated distinct variations of fluorescence and absorbance of GQDs/AuNPs system resulted from the interactions between protein species and sensing units contribute to a dual-signal strategy for protein discrimination. The protein concentration for complete discrimination is low down to 50 nM, and accurate discriminations of protein mixture of different concentrations/molar ratio are achieved. The complementary fluorescence and absorbance response makes this dual-signal model array sensor practicable to sample of complicated matrices, demonstrated by the accurate discrimination of protein species in human urine. Moreover, six strains of microorganisms originated from three different species are also successfully discriminated with 100% accuracy (OD600 = 1.0).

A dual-signal model array sensor is developed and applied for the highly sensitive discrimination of proteins and microorganisms based on GQDs stabilized AuNPs sensing system.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The discrimination and differentiation of proteins and microorganisms are gaining increasing attention in many fields including proteomics, medical diagnosis, and pathogenic detections. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is a methodology for protein assay based on the specific interactions between protein specie and receptor. The specific interaction usually offer the protein assay with high selectivity, while the practical application of ELISA to complex samples is limited due to the difficulty to provide multi specific interactions between different protein species and receptors at the same time. However, current situation in the field of medical diagnostics and life analysis put forward higher requirements for rapid, sensitive, cost-effective, high-throughput discrimination protocol. In this respect, the strategy of array sensing offers an effective solution. The array sensor is based on multiple selective but unspecific of sensing units (so-called ‘chemical tongue/nose’) [1–3], and provides multiple signals at the same time to meet the requirements of simultaneous multi-analyte assays. In the strategy of array sensing, each receptor gets different signals from various analytes, and meanwhile, each analyte offers different responses to all receptors, providing unique pattern for multi-analyte discrimination. This response pattern can be visually presented in the form of fingerprints by chemometric analysis [4]. Recently, array sensors exploring carbon nanotubes, nanoparticles, graphene oxide, polymers as sensing units have been successfully applied to the differentiation and identification of protein species [5–11]. In these sensing systems, the discrimination of multi-analyte is usually based on a single-signal strategy. That is, pluralities of optical probes are adopted as the signal receptors, and their sole optical response, i.e., fluorescence or absorbance, towards different analytes are collected for the ensuing discriminations. Therefore, the fabrications of multi probes to provide various spectral responses, which usually are complicated and time-consuming, become the prerequisite for the successful discrimination.

With the development of nanotechnology, the increasing appearance of nanomaterials with excellent optical properties provides a new opportunity for array sensor. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is a widely used metal nanomaterial merited with favorable characteristics such as size/distance-dependent optical property [12,13], enzyme-like catalytic activities [14–16], and outstanding affinity to biomolecules [17,18]. The unique characteristic resonance adsorption of AuNPs made it excellent sensing unit for colorimetric sensor arrays via the combination with fluorophores [2], aptamers [19], DNA [5,20,21] and polymers [22] etc.

Graphene oxide (GO) is a kind of 2D nanomaterial with excellent thermal, mechanical, and electronic properties, and widely applied in sensing field [7,11,21,23] as an effective fluorescence quencher. Fluorescent molecules can be adsorbed to the surface of GO by π-π stacking, hydrophobic and electrostatic interaction, leading to the fluorescence quenching. After the addition of analytes to the system, fluorescent molecules are separated from the surface of GO owing to the competitive activities between analytes and fluorescent molecules, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence [24–27]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a zero-dimensional carbon material with the size of less than 10 nm. Compared with GO of restricted optical properties, GQDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties due to the strong quantum confinement effect, which make it excellent optical probe and gain it wide applications in biological imaging [28], drugs delivery [29] and quantitative assay [30–32].

Herein we developed a GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor coupling the fluorescence property of GQDs with the plasmon resonance absorbance of AuNPs. The electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions among the targeted protein species and positively charged PEI-modified GQDs/negatively charged AuNPs give rise to diverse fluorescence and absorbance responses. The simultaneously generated variations of fluorescence and absorbance of GQDs/AuNPs upon the addition of proteins offer the dual-signal base for protein discrimination. Compared with the reported single-signal model array sensors for protein discrimination, only two sensing units are adopted in this developed array sensor. The fabrications of these two optical probes are simple [33], and no tedious functionalizations are needed. Moreover, the simultaneously trigged fluorescence variation from GQDs and absorbance variation from AuNPs upon the addition of protein contribute to a novel sensitive dual-signal strategy for protein discrimination, which not only leads to a identifiable protein concentration low down to 50 nM, but also offers the array sensor favorable anti-interference performance, i.e., the successful discrimination of protein species in complicated urine sample. This developed dual-signal model array sensor also can be used for microorganism discrimination based on the surface protein variations of different microorganisms. Six strains of microorganisms from three different species, i.e., E. Coli, P. Aeruginosa and B. Aubtilis are totally discriminated (OD600 = 1.0) by this dual-signal model array sensor.

Hemoglobin (Hb), myoglobin (Mb), transferrin (Trf), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and α-lactalbumin (α-La), hyperbranched polyethylenimine (PEI) are obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). High purity graphite powder, HAuCl4, C6H5Na3O7, H2SO4, K2S2O8, P2O5, KMnO4, H2O2 and BaCl2 are obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaNO3, HCl are purchased from Bodi Chemical Holding Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The reagents are at least of analytical reagent grade and used as received. All solutions are prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2 MΩ cm.

Graphene oxide (GO) is firstly prepared from natural graphite powder according to the Hummers and Offeman method [34]. The graphite powder is treated by pre-oxidation with P2O5, K2S2O8 and concentrated sulfuric acid before use.

0.050 g of GO is dispersed in 15 mL of deionized water, and then mixed with 10 mL of 25 mg mL−1 PEI. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture is transferred to a 150 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution is filtered with 0.45 μm PTFE Millipore filter. Afterward, the filtrate is dialyzed through a cellulose membrane (MWCO, 3500 g mol−1) for three days. The PEI-modified GQDs are dried in a vacuum freeze drier for 24 h.

AuNPs are prepared via the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 [35]. 500 μL of HAuCl4 (1%, m/v) is added to 50 mL deionized water and then heated to boiling. 570 μL of sodium citrate (1%, m/v) is quickly added to the boiling solution, and the solution is kept boiling for another 10 min. Then, the solution is cooled to room temperature and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Firstly, GQDs solution (1 mg mL−1) and AuNPs solution (Abs527 nm = 0.75) are mixed with a volume ratio of 1:5, and the mixed solution is shocked at 37 °C for 30 min. Then 675 μL of above solution is mixed with 33 μL of protein solution, and the resultant mixture is diluted to 3300 μL with deionized water. After shaking at 37 °C for 60 min, 200 μL of the above mixture is transferred into a 96-well plate for absorbance and fluorescence (λex/em = 340 nm/460 nm) determination by a Biotek Synergy H1 Microplate Reader. Each measurement is performed for six replicates. The raw data matrix is processed by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) in SPSS v17.0. For practical application, healthy human urine is collected and centrifuged at 12000 rpm to remove insoluble matrix. Finally, the urine is 100-fold diluted for proteins discrimination.

Microorganisms are cultured in LB broth at 250 rpm and 37 °C for 10 h, and then stored at 4 °C for ensuing use. Before the discrimination, the cultures are centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, washed with deionized water for three times, and then the concentration of microorganism is adjusted to OD600 of 1.0. Afterward, 660 μL of microorganism solution is added into the sensing system (GQDs/AuNPs), the mixture is diluted to 3300 μL with deionized water. The fluorescence response (λex/em = 340 nm/460 nm) and absorbance maximum at 527 nm with the addition of microorganism solutions are adopted as the output response.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-prepared AuNPs and GQDs are presented in Fig. 1 . The as-prepared AuNPs are of good monodispersed spherical (Fig. 1A), the distribution of size is uniform with an average size of 28 nm. HRTEM image of AuNPs (the inset of Fig. 1A) presents well-resolved lattice planes with a spacing of ∼0.23 nm, which is in accordance with the previous report [36]. TEM image of GQDs reveals that the nanoparticles are uniformly distributed, with average size of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The insets of Fig. 1B show the crystalline structure and size distribution histogram. The lattice spacing is ∼0.21 nm, matching the (100) lattice spacing of graphite [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation and identification of protein species
Differentiation and identification of different proteins
ce:section.ce:para: PEI and GQDs have been demonstrated to be excellent stabilizer for AuNPs preparation. The cationic charge density and steric effect of PEI can keep the formed AuNPs apart and leads to stable AuNPs colloid [38,39]. Oxygen containing groups of GQDs can effectively prevent AuNPs from aggregation, resulting in improved dispensability and stability of AuNPs in aqueous solution [40]. Therefore, in present study, PEI modified GQDs is firstly prepared and acted as the stabilizer for the formed AuNPs, which contribute to stable AuNPs colloid. This obtained GQDs/AuNPs system exhibits a clear surface plasmon absorption band centered at 527 nm and a fluorescence emission maximum of 460 nm (Fig. S1). With the addition of proteins into the colloid, the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions among GQDs, AuNPs and different protein species not only leads to the change of surface properties of GQDs, but also cause the variations of the existence status of AuNPs, which eventually results in the simultaneous changes of fluorescence and absorbance of GQDs-stabilized AuNPs system. The diverse variations of fluorescence and absorbance upon the addition of different protein species provide the base of dual-signal model array sensor for protein differentiation and identification, as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

In order to investigate the performance of GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor in protein differentiation and identification, six proteins with different molecular weight (MW), isoelectric point (pI), and metal/nonmetal containing properties are adopted as the discriminating targets (Table 1 ).

Firstly, the concentration ratio of sensing units is optimized by fixing the resonance absorbance of AuNPs colloid (λ = 527 nm) at 0.75 and changing the concentration of GQDs (0.75 mg mL−1, 1 mg mL−1, 1.25 mg mL−1). By the employment of linear discriminate analysis (LDA), the signal variations upon the addition of individual protein species are quantitatively analyzed to achieve the protein identification and differentiation. For each protein species, six replicated tests are performed, the obtained 72 raw signals (2 sensing signals × 6 proteins × 6 replicates) are subject to LDA to generate two canonical factors, which represent the linear combinations of fluorescence and absorbance response matrix. The two discrimination factors are employed to generate a 2D canonical score plot. Each point represents the response of the dual-signal model array sensor to each protein species. The obtained signals of 6 protein species are clustered into 6 distinct areas when 1 mg mL−1 GQDs is adopted (Fig. 3 A). The signal area of each protein is far from that of other protein species in the canonical score plot, offering a discrimination accuracy of 100%. While under the GQDs concentration of 0.75 mg mL−1 or 1.25 mg mL−1, lower discrimination accuracy is obtained as the signal areas of some proteins are close to that of others in LDA plot (Fig. S2). Therefore, 1 mg mL−1 of GQDs is adopted for protein discrimination in present study. Fig. 3B shows the fingerprints of six proteins at the concentration level of 500 nM, based on the patterns of the corresponding values deduced from fluorescence and absorbance changes of GQDs/AuNPs system, and the raw data and identification output are listed in Table S1. The tested protein species are obviously divided into two groups in canonical score plot under optimal ratio. Hb, Mb, HRP are vested in the first group, which cause the fluorescence quenching of this sensing system significantly. There are heme groups included in these three metalloproteins, which has been demonstrated to be an effective electron-transfer fluorescence quencher [11]. That is, the effective electron transfers from heme of Hb, Mb, HRP to GQDs lead to the fluorescence quenching of the sensing system. The other proteins including Trf, BSA and α-La (i.e.), which nearly pose any effect on the fluorescence of GQDs/AuNPs system, fall into the second group. Moreover, As the surface properties of involved protein species are different from each other, the varied affinity interaction between protein and AuNPs would cause the aggregation of AuNPs to different degree, which eventually results in different absorbance changes and diverse wavelength shifts of this sensing system, in case of the addition of various proteins.

Fig. 3C and D illustrate the results of LDA examinations based on GQDs/AuNPs sensor array under protein concentration of 100 nM and 50 nM. All six protein species can be completely discriminated with accuracy of 100% (Tables S2 and S3). When the concentration of proteins is down to 20 nM, signal overlapping of some protein species to each other are observed (Fig. S3, Table S4), due to the weak fluorescence and absorbance variations under low protein concentration.

The performance of this GQDs/AuNPs array sensor is also investigated by identifying protein mixture made of two proteins with various concentrations and molar ratio. Fig. 4 A shows the identifying results for the protein mixture of Hb and BSA at three different concentrations (50 nM, 100 nM and 500 nM). It is clear that the LDA plots of various protein concentrations respond to individual pattern respectively, and all proteins of different concentration are well differentiated from each other with an accuracy of 100%. The results of identifying the mixture of proteins at different molar ratios (Hb/BSA of 100/0, 70/30, 50/50, 10/90 and 0/100) are shown in Fig. 4B. These protein mixtures are completely distinguished from each other. These results well suggest that this GQDs/AuNPs array sensor is potential in differentiating protein samples of complex composition.

The composition of urine proteins is associated with kidney disease diagnosis [41]. However, there are complex inorganic salts and organic molecules in human urine. Therefore, it is highly challenging for the identification of proteins in human urine of complex matrixes. To further demonstrate the practical value of this dual-signal array sensor, the identification of urine proteins is performed. The discrimination result of blank (water), urine, and urine spiked with 6 previously-tested proteins are showed in Fig. 5 . In the canonical plot, the relevant proteins (500 nM) are totally separated from each other without overlapping with the signal of blank and urine, suggesting that this GQDs/AuNPs based dual-signal model array sensor are practical in the discrimination of proteins in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation and identification of unknown protein samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the robustness of this GQDs/AuNPs based dual-signal model array sensor, the aforementioned LDA results is taken as training sets (“training matrix”) for identification of unknown specimens. 18 unknown samples randomly taken from the training set at three concentration levels (500 nM, 100 nM, 50 nM) are identified by the dual-signal model array sensor, and the discrimination results are listed in Tables S5–S7. The results indicate that the identification accuracy rates of the unknown samples are all 100% at tested concentration levels. As the concentration of proteins is lower down to 50 nM, the discrimination accuracy for 18 unknown proteins is still remarkable, suggesting the great potential of this dual-signal array sensor in protein discrimination.

The differentiation of microorganisms is of great importance in many fields, especially in food analysis to ensure the food safety [42]. The performances of this GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor in differentiation and identification of microorganisms are thus investigated. Six strains of microorganisms from 3 different species, i.e., Escherichia coli (E.Coli O157:H7 and E. Coli ATCC35218), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P.Aeruginosa CICC10204 andP. Aeruginosa CICC21954), Bacillus subtilis (B.Aubtilis CICC10071 and B. Aubtilis CICC10275) are selected as the microorganism models. By solely evaluating the differences of size and charge property (Table S8), it is quite difficult to discriminate two strains of microorganism of same species from each other. However, it is reasonable to infer that the variations of protein species and their proportions on the surface of microorganism cells might provide different response patterns via appropriate sensing systems. Fig. 6 illustrates the results discriminated by GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model array sensor. All 6 microorganisms are significantly separated into six individual groups with discrimination accuracy of 100% at microorganism concentration level of 1.0 OD600. That is, different species can be markedly distinguished. It should be noticed that the microorganism species of similar origination, i.e., E. Coli O157:H7 and E. Coli ATCC35218, P. Aeruginosa CICC10204 and P. Aeruginosa CICC21954, B. Aubtilis CICC10071 and B. Aubtilis CICC10275, can also be differentiated apparently, even they share similar size and zeta potential. The observations demonstrate that the GQDs/AuNPs array sensor exhibits potentials in fields of biomedicine such as discrimination and identification of drug-resistant or pathogens strains.

In conclusion, a dual-signal model array sensor is developed and applied for the high sensitive discrimination of proteins and microorganisms based on GQDs stabilized AuNPs sensing system. The detection of the changes of fluorescence and absorbance response to this sensing system provides dual optical signatures for each protein species. The variations on the simultaneous generated fluorescence and absorbance response caused by each protein species brings this dual-signal model array sensor excellent protein discrimination ability, i.e., a detection limit low down to 50 nM is achieved. Compared with the previous reported single-signal “chemical tongue” strategy (0.2 μM-0.5 μM) [43,44], this present dual-signal protocol is merited with sufficiently high sensitivity. Moreover, the simultaneous detection of fluorescence and absorbance response provide complementary signal for protein discrimination, which offers this array sensor favorable anti-interference performance, and make it practical in the discrimination of physiologically relevant proteins in real-world sample, i.e., in complex matrix environment of human urine. The accurate discrimination of 6 strains of microorganisms from 3 different species further indicates that the GQDs/AuNPs-based dual-signal model sensing array owns promising potential for high-precision identification of biological targets, offering a new approach based on sensitive sensing platform for specific biomedical diagnostics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial supports from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21475017, 21275027, 21235010), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N150502001, N141008001).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.09.006.
